records.ety: [L. recessus, fr. recedere, recessum. See Recede.]

defs: A withdrawing or retiring; a moving back; retreat; as, the recess of the tides.
quotes: Every degree of ignorance being so far a recess and degradation from rationality.
My recess hath given them confidence that I may be conquered.

defs: The state of being withdrawn; seclusion; privacy.
quotes: In the recess of the jury they are to consider the evidence.
Good verse recess and solitude requires.

defs: Remission or suspension of business or procedure; intermission, as of a legislative body, court, or school; as, the children were allowed to play in the school yard during recess.
quotes: The recess of . . . Parliament lasted six weeks.

defs: Part of a room formed by the receding of the wall, as an alcove, niche, etc.
quotes: A bed which stood in a deep recess.

defs: A place of retirement, retreat, secrecy, or seclusion.
quotes: Departure from this happy place, our sweet Recess, and only consolation left.

defs: Secret or abstruse part; as, the difficulties and recesses of science; the deepest recesses of the mind.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Recessed; p. pr. & vb. n. Recessing.]

defs: To make a recess in; as, to recess a wall.
A decree of the imperial diet of the old German empire.
